content.text:
# Convertible Debt Instruments: Same vs. Different These terms are often used **somewhat interchangeably**, but there are meaningful distinctions. Here's a breakdown: ## The Umbrella Term **Convertible Debt** is the broad category—it refers to any debt instrument that can be converted into equity (shares). The others are specific *types* of convertible debt. ## The Specific Instruments | Term | Key Characteristics | |------|---------------------| | **Convertible Note** | • Typically **short-term**
• Often **unsecured**
• Common in **startups/early-stage** financing
• Usually simpler documentation | | **Convertible Bond** | • Typically **long-term**
• Often issued by **larger/established companies**
• Usually **secured** or backed by assets
• Traded on public markets | | **Convertible Debenture** | • A type of bond that is **unsecured** (backed only by issuer's creditworthiness)
• Long-term
• Relies on issuer reputation | ## Quick Summary ✅ **Same concept:** All represent borrowed money that can convert to equity ⚠️ **Key differences come down to:** - **Maturity** (notes = short-term; bonds/debentures = long-term) - **Security** (debentures are unsecured; bonds may be secured) - **Issuer type** (notes → startups; bonds → established firms) - **Formality/size** (bonds are larger, more standardized) ## Important Caveat > Usage **varies by region**. In some countries (like India or the UK), "debenture" is the standard term for corporate bonds. In the US, "bond" and "note" are more common. --- Would you like me to go deeper on any specific instrument or explain how the **conversion mechanics** work?
